question: What course modules are listed on the blackboard during the lecture, and what complexity class does the instructor say corresponds to problems solvable in polynomial time O(n^k)?
answer: The blackboard shows several modules including Divide & Conquer, Optimization (greedy and dynamic programming), Network Flow, Intractability with approximation, and Advanced Topics. The complexity class corresponding to problems solvable in polynomial time O(n^k) for some constant k is called P, which the instructor defines as the class of tractable problems.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard listing several course modules including Divide & Conquer, Optimization, Network Flow, Intractability
Instructor defines class P as problems solvable in polynomial time O(n^k)

question: What text does the blackboard display as the theme of today's lecture, and how does the instructor connect this theme to the interval scheduling problem introduced later in the same lecture?
answer: The blackboard displays 'Very similar problems can have very different complexity' as the theme of today's lecture. The instructor connects this theme to interval scheduling by showing that a basic interval scheduling problem can be solved in linear time using a greedy algorithm, adding weights changes it to a polynomial-time dynamic programming problem (O(n^2)), and adding non-identical machine constraints makes it NP-complete — demonstrating how small changes in problem statements lead to drastically different algorithmic complexities.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard shows 'Very similar problems can have very different complexity' as the theme
Instructor summarizes how interval scheduling, weighted interval scheduling, and non-identical machines represent the theme with different complexities

question: The blackboard shows 'Greedy Interval Scheduling' with three numbered steps. What are those three steps, and what selection rule does the instructor ultimately endorse as correct for this greedy algorithm?
answer: The three steps shown on the blackboard for the Greedy Interval Scheduling template are: (1) Use a simple rule to select a request i, (2) Reject all requests incompatible with i, and (3) Repeat until all requests are processed. The selection rule the instructor ultimately endorses as correct is the earliest finish time rule — selecting the request with the minimum finish time f(i) — which is proven to always produce the maximum number of compatible requests.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard displays the three-step Greedy Interval Scheduling template
Instructor confirms earliest finish time as the correct selection rule

question: What does the frame caption reveal about the blackboard content when NP-completeness is first formally defined, and how does the instructor relate the Hamiltonian cycle problem to this definition?
answer: The frame caption reveals that the blackboard displays 'NP-complete: problem is in NP and is as hard as any problem in NP' along with 'Hamiltonian cycle in a directed graph' and 'find a simple cycle that contains each vertex in V.' The instructor explains that the Hamiltonian cycle problem is NP-complete because it is both in NP (a given cycle can be verified in polynomial time by traversing and checking all vertices are visited exactly once) and is as hard as any problem in NP, meaning solving it in polynomial time would allow solving all NP problems in polynomial time.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard shows NP-complete definition and Hamiltonian cycle problem statement
Instructor explains verifiability of Hamiltonian cycle and its NP membership

question: What dynamic programming recursion formula appears on the blackboard for weighted interval scheduling, and what is the time complexity the instructor derives for this DP solution?
answer: The blackboard shows the recursion opt(R) = max over all i (Wi + opt(Rf(i))), where Wi is the weight of request i and Rf(i) is the set of requests with start time greater than or equal to f(i). The instructor derives a time complexity of O(n^2) for this dynamic programming solution, reasoning that there are n subproblems and each subproblem requires scanning through at most n requests, giving n times n equals n squared.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard displays opt(R) = max(Wi + opt(Rf(i))) recursion formula
Instructor derives O(n^2) complexity from n subproblems times n work each

question: What text is written on the blackboard instructing students about the course website, and what penalty does the instructor describe for students who do not attempt problem sets?
answer: The blackboard displays 'Please read Course Information document on website,' instructing students to consult course materials online. Regarding penalties, the instructor explains that while problem sets are only 30% of the grade, there is actually a penalty associated with not attempting problems and not turning in problem sets that is far greater than 30%, making it crucial for students to at minimum attempt every problem set.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard shows 'Please read Course Information document on website'
Instructor explains penalty for not attempting problems exceeds 30% of grade

question: What does the frame caption describe appearing on the blackboard when the Dynamic Programming section begins, and how does the instructor define the subproblems Rx for the weighted interval scheduling DP?
answer: The frame caption describes the blackboard showing 'Dynamic Programming' as a title along with the equation Rx equals the set of requests j in R such that s(j) is greater than or equal to x, together with the word 'Subproblems.' The instructor defines the subproblems by choosing x to be the finishing times f(i) of each request, so Rf(i) is the set of requests whose start time is greater than or equal to f(i) — meaning all requests that come strictly later than the finish of request i — resulting in exactly n subproblems, one for each request.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption shows blackboard with 'Dynamic Programming' and Rx set definition
Instructor explains x = f(i) gives Rf(i) as requests later than f(i), yielding n subproblems

question: According to the frame captions describing the blackboard, what is the formal definition of the class NP as written in this lecture?
answer: According to the frame captions, the blackboard writes that NP is the class of problems verifiable in polynomial time, specifically shown as 'NP: class of problems verifiable in poly time.' This means a proposed solution to a problem in NP can be checked for correctness in polynomial time, even if finding the solution itself may require exponential time.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption shows blackboard defining NP as class of problems verifiable in poly time

question: Based on the frame captions describing the blackboard at the end of the lecture, what scheduling problem variant is labeled NP-complete, and what distinguishes it from the basic interval scheduling problem?
answer: According to the frame captions, the blackboard at the end of the lecture labels the non-identical machines scheduling problem as NP-complete. The notation shown includes 'Non-Identical machines,' 'Y = {T1, ..., Tm},' and 'NP-complete,' indicating that what distinguishes this variant is that each request i has a constraint Q(i) specifying a subset of machines it can run on, as opposed to the basic single-resource interval scheduling where any request can use the single shared resource.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption shows blackboard with Non-Identical machines problem labeled NP-complete with Q(i) constraint

question: How does the instructor prove the claim that the greedy algorithm with earliest finish time produces the maximum number of intervals, and what specific inductive structure is used in the proof?
answer: The instructor proves the claim by induction on k*, the size of the optimal schedule. The base case is k* = 1, which is trivial since any single interval works. For the inductive step, assuming the claim holds for k*, the instructor considers a problem whose optimal schedule S* has k*+1 intervals. The proof constructs S** by replacing the first interval of S* with the greedy algorithm's first choice i1 (which has finish time no later than j1 of the optimal), defines L' as all intervals with start time at least f(i1), shows S**[2 to k*+1] is optimal for L', invokes the inductive hypothesis to conclude the greedy on L' produces k* intervals, and therefore the full greedy schedule has k*+1 intervals matching the optimal.

ground_truth_spans.description: Instructor states proof is by induction on k* and describes base case
Instructor constructs S** substituting greedy first choice into optimal schedule
Instructor invokes inductive hypothesis on L' to complete the proof

question: What two specific greedy heuristics are proposed by students during the lecture that the instructor demonstrates do NOT always produce the maximum compatible subset, and what counter-examples does the instructor provide for each?
answer: Two incorrect greedy heuristics proposed were: first, selecting requests in numerical order, which the instructor immediately rejects by pointing to the existing diagram showing that numerical order would fail to produce the maximum; second, selecting the request with the shortest duration (smallest f(i) - s(i)), which the instructor refutes by describing a counter-example of two non-overlapping requests with one very short request placed in the middle — selecting the shortest (middle) request precludes selecting both outer requests, yielding only one selection instead of the optimal two.

ground_truth_spans.description: Student proposes numerical order; instructor shows counter-example from diagram
Student proposes shortest duration; instructor provides counter-example with middle short interval

question: What are the prerequisites for 6.046 as described by the instructor at the start of the lecture, and what is the stated purpose of the Stellar website for running the course?
answer: The instructor states that 6.006 is a prerequisite for 6.046, and students who skipped it by exemption should check in with the course staff to verify they are adequately prepared. The assumed prerequisite knowledge includes basic data structures, classical algorithms such as sorting, dynamic programming algorithms, and shortest path algorithms. Stellar is described as the one-stop shop for the course, used for obtaining lecture handouts, problem sets, turning in problem sets, signing up for recitation sections, and accessing the course information and objectives documents.

ground_truth_spans.description: Instructor describes 6.006 as prerequisite and lists required background knowledge
Instructor describes Stellar as one-stop shop for course materials and submissions

question: What formal definition of two requests being compatible is given in the interval scheduling problem, and what does the instructor say about intervals where f(i) equals s(j)?
answer: Two requests i and j are defined as compatible if they do not overlap, formally stated as f(i) less than or equal to s(j) or f(j) less than or equal to s(i). The instructor specifically notes that when f(i) equals s(j) — meaning the finish time of one interval exactly equals the start time of the next — the two requests are still considered compatible. This is because the instructor uses open-ended intervals on the right-hand side for f(i), so touching endpoints are allowed and do not constitute an overlap.

ground_truth_spans.description: Instructor defines compatibility condition including the case where f(i) equals s(j) being compatible

question: What specific reason does the instructor give for why the greedy algorithm template fails for the weighted interval scheduling problem, and what algorithmic paradigm does the instructor endorse as the solution?
answer: The instructor states that for weighted interval scheduling, there is no simple selection rule that can be plugged into the greedy template to guarantee the maximum weight solution for every problem instance. The instructor demonstrates this with a trivial counter-example: three intervals where two non-overlapping intervals each have weight 1, and one central interval has a very large weight (described as infinity), showing that earliest finish time would greedily pick the two small-weight intervals instead of the single high-weight one. The instructor endorses dynamic programming as the correct algorithmic paradigm for this problem.

ground_truth_spans.description: Instructor explains greedy template fails for weighted case and endorses dynamic programming with counter-example
